title: Modularity in Weight (1,1,...,1) via Overconvergent Hilbert Modular Forms
speaker: Payman Kassaei

proof_stages.label: Buzzard-Taylor deformation-to-overconvergent setup
proof_stages.summary: The speaker recalls the Buzzard-Taylor strategy: from an ordinary two-dimensional p-adic Galois representation with residual modularity, R=T methods produce two overconvergent weight-one eigenforms f and g with the same Hecke eigenvalues away from p and U_p-eigenvalues alpha and beta.

proof_stages.label: Modular-curve analytic continuation and gluing
proof_stages.summary: On the rigid modular curve with Gamma_0(p)-level, valuation domains and the Atkin-Lehner involution are used to glue alpha f minus beta g to w(f minus g), giving analytic continuation to the whole curve and hence classicality and descent to level prime to p.

proof_stages.label: Hilbert modular theorem and inert obstruction
proof_stages.summary: The Hilbert modular weight-one modularity theorem is stated, the split case is cited, and the problem is reduced to the inert case, where the natural continuation domain U and its Atkin-Lehner translate have too small a union because of vertical components in the special fiber.

proof_stages.label: Mod-p Hilbert modular geometry and valuation dictionary
proof_stages.summary: The special fibers of the Hilbert modular varieties X and Y are described via type strata and the Goren stratification indexed by cube faces, yielding partial and total valuation maps and the connectedness information needed for later gluing.

proof_stages.label: Analytic continuation to the enlarged region R
proof_stages.summary: An enlarged region R is defined using type of cardinality at most one and a total-valuation bound, and the functional equation f = alpha^{-1} U_p f together with Kisin-module calculations for the Hecke correspondence shows that nonzero-U_p overconvergent Hilbert modular forms extend to R.

proof_stages.label: Gluing through a saturated middle domain
proof_stages.summary: Using an auxiliary correspondence with maps pi_1 and pi_2, q-expansions, connectedness of the R_A pieces, and a saturated subdomain C, the pulled-back identity is upgraded to alpha f minus beta g equals w(f minus g) on R intersect wR, so the form glues over R union wR.

proof_stages.label: Descent and Koecher classicality
proof_stages.summary: Since R union wR still has codimension-one holes upstairs, the glued form is descended from the type-at-most-one locus of Y to the corresponding locus of X using the saturated domain C/C_0 and trace, then extended across a codimension-at-least-two complement by a rigid Koecher principle to obtain a classical level-prime-to-p form.

proof_stages.label: Scope under ramification and related work
proof_stages.summary: The speaker explains how the same gluing and descent issues change in unramified, slightly ramified, tamely ramified, and wildly ramified settings, citing related work of Pilloni and joint work with Sasaki and Tian.

examples.name: Degree-two inert Hilbert modular surface picture
examples.note: For g=2 with p inert, the cube becomes a square with horizontal and vertical components, making visible why U union wU misses tubes and how the enlarged region R is positioned.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker draws and reasons through the degree-two inert Hilbert modular surface, identifying four component types, vertical versus horizontal components, the regions missed by U∪wU, and the later R-region/type picture.

passes.reason: A quadratic-inert Hilbert modular surface is a typical low-dimensional Hilbert modular variety used to show the naive U_can ∪ wU_can continuation misses vertical-component tubes.
The real-quadratic inert Γ0(p) Hilbert modular surface is a representative low-dimensional member, used to show the naive U∪wU continuation/gluing region misses tubes of vertical components.

The degree-two inert Hilbert modular surface is used as a representative low-dimensional illustration of the stratification/vertical-component obstruction, not to refute a claim, and it is not structurally degenerate, extremal, sporadic, or maximally symmetric.

A real-quadratic inert Γ0(p) Hilbert modular surface is an ordinary member of an infinite family, but here its vertical components show that U∪wU misses tubes, obstructing the naive analytic-continuation argument.

The degree-two inert Hilbert modular surface is an ordinary low-dimensional case in the Hilbert modular variety family, used to exhibit the obstruction that the naive regions U and wU miss tubes of vertical components.

The degree-2 inert Hilbert modular surface is a standard low-dimensional member of the Hilbert modular family, used to show the naive analytic-continuation cover fails because vertical-component tubes are missed.
The real-quadratic inert Γ0(p) Hilbert modular surface is an ordinary member of its family, used to show the naive U∪wU continuation/gluing region misses tubes of vertical components.

The degree-two inert Hilbert modular surface picture is used to show the naive region U∪wU misses tubes of vertical components, obstructing the continuation argument, while the surface itself is just a representative low-dimensional Hilbert modular case.

used as an obstruction to the naive gluing/analytic-continuation argument since in the inert degree-two picture U_can∪W(U_can) misses tubes of vertical components, while the surface itself is a standard low-dimensional Hilbert modular variety rather than structurally special

The degree-2 inert Hilbert modular surface is used to exhibit the vertical-component obstruction where the natural region and its Atkin--Lehner image fail to cover enough, while structurally it is an ordinary low-dimensional member of the Hilbert modular variety family.
A standard g=2 inert Hilbert modular Gamma0(p) surface picture is used to exhibit the obstruction that the naive continued regions U_can and WU_can miss tubes of vertical components.
A standard g=2 inert Hilbert modular surface case is used to show the obstruction that the natural continuation region and its Atkin–Lehner image miss tubes of vertical components.
